speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was carrying clothes to the-- disinfection-- disinfected clothes back to a place in Auschwitz to be cleaned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were the clothes coming from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. They must have come from dead people. I don't know. But they wanted to have these clothes saved and-- from the inmates, naturally-- the dead inmates. I didn't see that. But the clothes was there in a big [? room, ?] and a few people were chosen by the blockova to take it back to the crematorium territory, and we did. And when I was there I saw the [? colliding ?] room, but is already cleaned rags, so I did go in, and I took a lot of clothes. I put them on myself, because you couldn't carry bundles with you. And that was August-- very, very hard. But I was successful. I brought quite a few clothes. And when I came back to the barracks, I gave it away to different people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it that you were not caught?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there were more girls who were [INAUDIBLE], and, I mean, we brought the clothes. And we were going back to the barracks. Nobody checked us. I don't know how. I didn't even want to find out probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you do this job for?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I just did it a few times. That was just a moment decision from the blockova whom she will sent. That was not a steady job. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You never know who will do that. And then I gave it away, so some of the people said that I saved their life, because they could use some of the clothes in different ways-- as a blanket, or-- or as a long skirt, or as a cape, the very same piece of clothes. That was one. The other one we did that-- some man from the men barracks, what was very far away from us-- I don't know where-- came to cut off-- off squared piece of earth, and we had to carry it to the SS men barracks, because they loved flowers. And they wanted to have a garden. We sat the problem was that we couldn't straighten up as we were cutting this piece of earth, because it was very, very heavy. And if you dropped it, you were lucky if you were not shot, but you definitely were punished.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of punishment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beaten or not getting your bread or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you cope on a day-to-day basis with all the threats of being killed or being beaten? What did you do to avoid that? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I cannot give you a-- a straight answer because, first of all, we didn't think about how I will cope with it. Naturally, we coped with it because we had no other choice. Evening, when we finally huddled together and wanted to go to sleep, we naturally talked about all the things what was going through our mind. We talked about home and about our parents. We described exactly every detail about them and our sisters and brothers. We cooked what we never did at home, but, now we all cooked. We talked about food, because we were hungry nonstop. And in wintertime, we were terrible cold and freezing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were just talking. Maybe that was the only way we coped with it. I-- I cannot think any other way [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that we could do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any special dreams you had while you were in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In Auschwitz-- in Auschwitz dream?

speaker: interviewer
text: Or at any point in the concentration camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: In concentration camps-- dreams, we had a million. But in Braunschweig-- that was the city where we had to clean the main road. We were taken to Braunschweig from Auschwitz through Bergen-Belsen to work. And we had to clean the main road. There was very often air raids at that time. I was very sick then. I had typhus. But I wouldn't stay inside in the barracks, because that meant I stayed there and never come out alive. So when I was cleaning, I noticed a hole on the ground that was a shelter, an opening in to a shelter.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I walked down there, and there was a couch. I laid down and fell asleep. And I dreamed about my mother calling me, telling me that my father is calling. I should go to my father. I didn't want to go because I was so cozy and warm, but naturally I did go when my mother repeated it the second time that I should go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I came out from the hole, I saw the whole city completely empty-- airplane flying above the city. I never was so happy than then. Bombs falling from the plane, and I was really starting to dance. I was very happy. I knew the rest of the people are in the church where the SS men always took us in case of air raids.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was half-bombed already. The SS men all disappeared. They never were around when there was an air raid. They were in the shelter. So when I came to the church, my girlfriend, Gizko, said, where did you go? You know that if the SS men see you, they will shoot you on the spot. But-- What kind of significance did that dream have for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of significance did that dream have for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my mother saved me.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in concentration camp, did you ever see any of your family members again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Auschwitz. I was still in Auschwitz and trying to stay there, because I heard that my sister is in Auschwitz finally, I found out, and coming to the next barracks, that is not far from where I was. And there is an empty lot, no electrified wire between the barracks and an [? odd ?] situation, just an SS man and a German shepherd who were patrolling the side.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I finally saw my sister coming, I run over to her. And I hugged her. She said, I should eat everything, because we have to tell to the world what's going on when we will be free. And we hugged each other. She pushed me back. She said, go back. She knew what does that mean. I wasn't yet familiar so much with Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I came back, my girlfriends told me that the SS men told to the dog to go after me, but the dog didn't move. And now, when I came back, the dog and the SS man still were frozen. They still didn't move. So I never was punished for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Clara, Clare, or Claire.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think she was 28 or 29. Something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see her again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was the last time when I saw her. She was taken to Bergen-Belsen, as I found out about 3-4 years later from somebody who was with her. And three days earlier, as the British soldiers liberated Bergen-Belsen, she was shot by the German SS. They were line-- lined up every inmate, and every 10 were shot. That was a game-- their entertainment-- to shoot every 10 inmates and then starting all of it again-- the counting. And that's the way my sister died.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Auschwitz for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Auschwitz five months, and then I was taken to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you taken there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did go with train, and a very short way we walked. I don't know from where to where. That was horrible, and Auschwitz was horrible. The snow-- the rain was falling all the time. Everything was knee high mud, like in Auschwitz, where we left. When we left Auschwitz, it was the same way. And every single day, hundreds and hundreds of inmates died. We were put into a tent because they had not enough space in the barracks anymore. And then we finally came into the barracks, but from there we were taken to work-- to Braunschweig, as I mentioned to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, what did you do to maintain your dignity as a human being at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: What kept me? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know what-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what keep you. You know, we all-- the 10 girls I am talking about-- and the rest of the group whom I was together, we never lost our dignity or human beings. We always-- you know, the Germans wanted to make us inhuman. They wanted to take our spirit, not only our-- our energy and life. But they never could, because we always tried to help each other. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] when we saw something is not right-- one thing that happened also in Braunschweig, that when we were standing in line for a piece of bread, that was a torture every night, because we were watching what size, what piece of the bread will come to us. You know, as we stood in line, you can-- you could see in the basket. And finally, I saw that an end will be my piece. And a blockova took it out and gave it to me. But when-- before I could even take, she looked at me and took it back and gave it to a girl who was behind me. And I got very angry-- in concentration camp, I got angry-- and I said, that is my bread and give me that. She offered me a smaller piece. And I said, that other one is what is mine. And she said, you don't want to have that, you don't get any. So she didn't give me any. But things like that happened to me, and there was the coffee cup.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened with the coffee cup?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of-- we were cleaning the street, and we found many times some kind of broken dishes or coffee cups, or whatever. My friend found a coffee cup. And every morning, when we had the so-called coffee, she poured a little coffee down into that cup and gave it to me, because I loved that cup. And as I was drinking it, the blockova came and saw the cup, and she said, I want to have that cup. She was counting us. When she left, I gave back my cup-- the cup-- to my friend. And the blockova asked me for the cup when she returned. So I said, I don't have the cup. That's my friend's. And I cannot give it to her.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she got very angry. She slapped me on the face. I fell for a while. And I didn't get breakfast the next day-- I mean, the coffee. And evening I didn't get the bread and the soup, so-called soup. And that was my punishment because I didn't give the coffee cup, but I wouldn't because that was not mine. What ever happened with the cup?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other woman who gave it to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I gave it back to her, and she kept it as long as we could. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever any competition or fighting amongst the inmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: For what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Over things like food or anything of that sort?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not with my group. I don't know if any other place. But, no, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no fighting. I was fighting with the blockova. I mean, I told her that it's not justice that she gives my portion to the other person. But I didn't fight with the person who got the bigger piece. I mean, I didn't say anything to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever in any other camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I was in many camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall the names of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was in Auschwitz for five months. And then we were taken to Bergen-Belsen. From Bergen-Belsen, we were taken to Braunschweig. And then other one, where we were. And then we worked in a so-called airplane factory--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the name of that place?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- and where we chose the screws from the bad to the good. We selected it all. The only things I could do and many others, I am sure, that we took the good one and threw it out, and we selected the bad one and put it in to the good box. That was the only thing we could do against the German. You follow me? And then, from there, we did go several-- several other places-- so-called camp, like Buchenwald, but not in to the Buchenwald camp, but outskirt of Buchenwald. And then we did go in the open-- open train. No-- they had not even a roof over our head. And that was April, 1945. And the SS ordered us to come off from the train. When we did, they lined us up before the machine guns. In that time-- two weeks-- we didn't get any food. We ate the grass and the leaves from the tree. And the rain-- when was falling, we'd drink the rain water. And then an airplane flew over the place where we were, dropping-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] now I forgot, suddenly, the name-- anyway, leaflet telling to the German not to kill us. And the German were telling us not to pick it up, the paper, but we did. So all the SS men, except two run away. The remaining two put-- ordered back to the train, and we were going all over. They didn't know what to do with us, but they didn't want to leave us behind, because they were afraid whoever will liberate that part, you know, because we didn't know, but they did, that the Americans were already there, surrounding them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your liberation date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think 1st of May, or 2nd of May in [NON-ENGLISH], when the Red Cross from Sweden came over and took us over and took us to Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to jump ahead now. I'd like you to talk to me about some of your recent achievements with your Holocaust work, some of the places that you've been working with or who maybe you've shared your testimony with.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am speaking almost 20 years now to the-- with the Wiesenthal Center-- I mean, for the Wiesenthal Center. I did go to colleges and schools, churches. I did go to Sacramento to the Senate and to the Assemblymen. I was-- spoke in there. And I made many, many tapes, all free, all volunteer job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't do it for the money. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: For many-- I gave many interviews for newspapers and tapes, and so on. The latest one is the '7th Heaven' TV show, who made an episode of hatred. And they did use a part of my life story.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came to the Wiesenthal Center, or called the Wiesenthal Center, and asked whom they would recommend, a survivor, whose story should be filmed or written, because they wanted to write about hatred, and they wanted to have a survivor's, not an imaginary person's, story.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they wanted to have an authentic people. And that's the way I met the writer and told my story. And she wrote the story and made it to her show. What was that particular experience like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: For me, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that was odd. I mean, first I didn't want to do. But I am very happy I did, because-- excuse me-- because that way, many school children and grownups also, but mainly school children, can see what hatred really means and where that can go. Like in the show, the little girl is envying another girl, and because of that, they hate that person. And by the end, they came to my story, seeing that this hatred can grow in grownups so much that they really can hurt people. So I am very happy that tape was made.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of message do you tell to the people that you talk to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to let people know that we should work for peace and fight for peace, and peace will never be if we don't make it possible. I believe there could be peace in the whole world. And I would like to give the message to stop hatred. It doesn't matter [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] against whom the hatred goes-- against the Jews or against blacks or Orientals or whoever. Hatred is hatred, and we have to stop it. Not with guns, but education and understanding and patience, because you have to give a chance to a person to understand what really hatred means and does to everyone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you would like to add to your testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: To my testimony? I don't know. One thing that I usually say is that Hitler hated the Jews and ordered the Jews to be shot, and then put gasoline over them and burned, or in the gas chamber. It doesn't matter where. And after 12 years, the full circle got on him, and 1945, April 30th, when Hitler finally believed that the war is over, and he lost, he told his ministers that he will shoot himself, and they should pour gasoline over his body and burn him. And that's exactly what happened to him. And I think that is a very good message for people who are hating and giving, you know, orders to other people to be killed, to see where they end up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there something else that you wanted to stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry. And I would like to just add something-- that regardless, I did go through concentration camp and all the tremendous disappointment what I suffered from people and from life. I still believe the majority of peoples are good. And there are evil people, and they are the one that we have to stop. And that is the job for the good people, because they will win by the end. Thank you.
